name: Hanging chain (catenary)
description: A chain suspended from two fixed points and left to hang under gravity settles into a curve that exactly realizes the hyperbolic cosine. Gaudí used physical catenaries (inverted) to design the arches of the Sagrada Família.
conditions_of_correctness: chain is inextensible and perfectly flexible
gravity only — no wind, no additional load
breaks_when: Non-uniform load → weighted catenary. Elastic chain → Euler elastica. Horizontal uniform load → parabola (suspension bridge approximation).

name: Soap film
description: A soap film spanning a closed wire boundary settles into the surface of minimum area — the solution to Plateau's problem. For two parallel rings it realizes a catenoid. Can approximate Steiner trees for planar point sets.
breaks_when: Multiple local minima exist — film may settle into a non-global minimum. Evaporation introduces drift.

name: Marble adding machine
description: Gravity-fed marble runs with rocker/seesaw gates implement binary arithmetic. The Digi-Comp II (1965) is the canonical design. One marble = 1 bit. The rocker flips state on each pass, implementing a half-adder at each stage.
conditions_of_correctness: all marbles same size and mass
rocker pivot friction calibrated — flips reliably, no bounce
breaks_when: Simultaneous marble arrival causes collision at rocker. Worn pivot loses reliable flip.

diy.label: Binary Marbles — build your own guide

name: K'NEX marble computer
description: K'NEX construction set pieces used to build working marble-run logic gates and counters. The modular snap-fit system makes it easy to prototype and reconfigure gate layouts without custom woodworking.
conditions_of_correctness: tracks level on a flat surface
breaks_when: Loose connectors allow track flex, causing missed triggers or double-flips.

name: Domino computer
description: Standing dominoes propagate a falling signal. Fan-outs split signals, and careful geometry implements AND and OR gates. Signal is one-shot — must reset by standing dominoes again.
conditions_of_correctness: uniform domino spacing for reliable propagation
breaks_when: Signal dies at a gap. Simultaneous paths must be timed to meet at AND gate.
speed: ~1 domino per second propagation (~10-50 seconds total)

name: Galton board (bean machine)
description: Balls dropped through a triangular array of pegs deflect left or right at each level. The distribution of balls in the output bins converges to a Gaussian as N→∞. Each peg is an independent Bernoulli trial.
breaks_when: Small N → binomial approximation only. Ball-ball collisions at high density introduce correlations.
speed: minutes to hours (depending on ball count)
compute_capacity: statistical sampling (scales with number of balls)

name: Physarum polycephalum (slime mold)
description: The plasmodial slime mold extends filaments toward nutrient sources and progressively reinforces paths that carry more flow, pruning inefficient routes. Toshiyuki Nakagaki showed it reproduces the Tokyo rail network topology.
breaks_when: Gets stuck in local optima. Result is heuristic — no guarantee of global optimum.

name: Simulated annealing (thermal)
description: A physical system coupled to a heat bath at slowly decreasing temperature explores its energy landscape. At high temperature it escapes local minima; as T→0 it settles into a global minimum — if cooling is slow enough.
conditions_of_correctness: cooling schedule slow enough (logarithmic in theory)
system ergodic — can reach any state from any other
breaks_when: Cooling too fast → gets trapped in local minimum. Non-ergodic energy landscape → misses global optimum.
speed: minutes to hours (depends on cooling schedule)
compute_capacity: global optimization problems (scales exponentially with problem size)

name: Slide rule
description: Logarithmic scales engraved on sliding rules allow multiplication by physical addition of lengths (log a + log b = log ab). Precision is bounded by engraving quality and human reading resolution — typically 3 significant figures.
conditions_of_correctness: reading at center of rule (error increases toward ends)
breaks_when: Result falls off scale edge. Reading error dominates for more than 3 sig figs.

name: Billiard-ball computer
description: Proposed by Fredkin & Toffoli (1982). Balls travel on paths representing wires; presence/absence of a ball encodes a bit. Collisions at path intersections implement logic gates. Logically and thermodynamically reversible — no information is destroyed.
breaks_when: Any friction or inelastic collision destroys reversibility and introduces errors. Practically unrealizable at macro scale.

name: MONIAC (Phillips hydraulic computer)
description: Built by Bill Phillips (1949). Water flows through tanks and pipes representing economic sectors — income, consumption, taxation, investment. Flow rates encode economic quantities. The system settles into equilibrium representing GDP balance. 14 machines were built.
conditions_of_correctness: pipe diameters calibrated to economic parameters
model assumptions hold (closed economy, linear flows)
breaks_when: Non-linear economic effects not representable in pipe geometry. Model is Keynesian — inapplicable outside those assumptions.

name: DigiComp II
description: Educational plastic computer kit released by E.S.R. Inc in 1968. Uses gravity-fed marbles through flip-flop gates to perform binary addition, multiplication, and other operations. Features 3 flip-flops that can store 3 bits of state, with operations performed by releasing marbles sequentially.
conditions_of_correctness: tube angles correct for reliable flow
breaks_when: Simultaneous marble arrival causes jamming. Worn plastic flip-flops may not toggle reliably. Dust or debris blocks marble paths.

diy.label: Evil Mad Scientist - DigiComp II kit
